scope_note: A legal-records and misuse-of-power research set covering selected high-salience matters from Trump's business career through his second administration. Entries distinguish adjudicated findings from allegations, investigative conclusions, impeachment judgments, dismissed charges, lawful but controversial exercises of power, and pending appeals. Summaries use primary legal and government records wherever practicable and do not treat an indictment, congressional referral, pardon, or investigative report as a criminal conviction.

items.title: Trump University fraud litigation ended in a $25 million settlement without an admission of wrongdoing
items.status_label: Settlement affirmed; no admission of wrongdoing
items.summary: After years of consumer-fraud litigation over Trump University, the parties reached a $25 million settlement: $21 million for the federal class actions and $4 million for the New York attorney general's case. The Ninth Circuit affirmed final approval in 2018. The agreement expressly did not require Trump or the other defendants to admit wrongdoing.
items.why_it_matters: The settlement returned an estimated 80 to 90 percent of eligible students' payments and resolved a major case alleging that Trump's branded educational venture used deceptive sales practices. It is strong evidence of the scale and cost of the dispute, but not an adjudication that Trump personally committed fraud.

evidence.label: Simpson v. Trump, Ninth Circuit opinion affirming settlement approval
evidence.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

items.editorial_note: Describe the underlying claims as allegations resolved by settlement. Do not say this settlement was a fraud verdict or an admission by Trump.

items.title: A New York court found that Trump breached fiduciary duties in his charitable foundation
items.status_label: Adjudicated civil fiduciary breach; foundation dissolved
items.summary: The Donald J. Trump Foundation dissolved under court supervision in December 2018. In November 2019, a New York judge found that Donald Trump had breached fiduciary duties and ordered him to pay $2 million in damages, directing that the money go to charities. The foundation's remaining assets had already been distributed to approved charities.
items.why_it_matters: This is an adjudicated example of Trump using a charitable entity without the basic separation and governance required of a fiduciary. The court record is more probative than generalized criticism because it identifies a legal duty, a breach, and a monetary remedy.

evidence.label: Matter of People v. Trump, decision and order
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

evidence.label: Attorney General summary of court order, dissolution, and governance remedies
evidence.publisher: Office of the New York Attorney General

items.editorial_note: The $2 million was court-ordered damages, not a criminal fine. Keep the finding focused on Donald Trump's fiduciary breach; describe remedies involving his children only as the record supports.

items.title: Civil judgments hold Trump liable for sexually abusing and repeatedly defaming E. Jean Carroll
items.status_label: $5 million judgment affirmed, with certiorari denied and a rehearing petition pending; $83.3 million judgment affirmed with Supreme Court review not exhausted as of access
items.summary: A federal jury found Trump civilly liable for sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll and defaming her, awarding $5 million. The Second Circuit affirmed. The Supreme Court denied certiorari on June 29, 2026, and Trump filed a petition for rehearing on July 8 that remained pending as of July 13. A second jury awarded $83.3 million for separate defamatory statements; the Second Circuit affirmed that judgment in September 2025 and denied rehearing in April 2026. As of July 13, 2026, Trump had announced but had not yet exhausted Supreme Court review of the second judgment.
items.why_it_matters: The judgments provide adjudicated civil findings concerning sexual misconduct and a sustained campaign of public defamation. They directly test attempts to dismiss the conduct as merely offensive rhetoric: juries found legally compensable injury, and appellate courts upheld the verdicts reviewed so far.

evidence.label: Second Circuit opinion affirming the $5 million verdict
evidence.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit via GovInfo

evidence.label: Supreme Court docket, Trump v. Carroll, No. 25-573
evidence.publisher: Supreme Court of the United States

evidence.label: Second Circuit opinion affirming the $83.3 million defamation judgment
evidence.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit via Justia

items.editorial_note: This was civil, not criminal, liability. The first jury found sexual abuse but did not find rape under the specific New York statutory definition presented on the verdict form. Preserve that distinction even when describing the court's later explanation of the term in ordinary usage.

items.title: New York appellate judges left Trump's civil-fraud liability intact but vacated the massive disgorgement award
items.status_label: Liability and nonmonetary relief affirmed; disgorgement vacated; further review unresolved in sources reviewed
items.summary: A New York trial court found persistent fraud in financial statements used in business transactions and entered broad relief, including hundreds of millions of dollars in disgorgement. On August 21, 2025, the Appellate Division affirmed the liability findings and nonmonetary relief but unanimously vacated the disgorgement awards as excessive under the Eighth Amendment. The sources reviewed do not establish a final resolution of further appellate proceedings.
items.why_it_matters: The surviving liability findings document a repeated pattern of materially false asset valuations rather than a single verbal exaggeration. At the same time, the appellate reversal of the monetary award is essential context and prevents the site from presenting the original dollar judgment as still operative.

evidence.label: Trial court decision and judgment in People v. Trump
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

evidence.label: Appellate Division decision modifying the judgment
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

items.editorial_note: Never quote the vacated disgorgement award as money Trump currently owes. The accurate present formulation is that liability survived while the monetary disgorgement remedy did not.

items.title: A New York jury convicted Trump on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records
items.status_label: Convicted and sentenced to unconditional discharge; direct appeal perfected and pending
items.summary: On May 30, 2024, a Manhattan jury found Trump guilty on 34 counts of falsifying business records in the first degree. The court entered judgment and imposed an unconditional discharge on January 10, 2025, meaning no incarceration, fine, or probation. Court records show that Trump perfected his direct appeal in 2026; the conviction therefore remains under appellate review.
items.why_it_matters: This is the only entry in this set based on a criminal jury conviction of Trump himself. The unusual sentence did not erase the guilty verdict, but the pending appeal means the site should not call the conviction final and unreviewable.

evidence.label: New York courts public case page for People v. Donald J. Trump
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

evidence.label: January 3, 2025 sentencing decision describing the 34-count verdict
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

evidence.label: Appellate Division order identifying the perfected appeal
evidence.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

items.editorial_note: State plainly that the appeal is pending. An unconditional discharge is a sentence without custody, fine, or supervision; it is not an acquittal or dismissal.

items.title: The Mueller report documented obstruction evidence but made no prosecutorial judgment against a sitting president
items.status_label: Investigative report; no obstruction charge and no adjudication
items.summary: Special Counsel Robert Mueller's second report volume analyzed multiple episodes in which Trump sought to influence or constrain the Russia investigation. Mueller explained that the Justice Department's policy against indicting a sitting president shaped the office's approach: it did not make a traditional charging decision. Mueller also said that if investigators had confidence Trump clearly did not commit a crime, the report would have said so, and emphasized that the report did not exonerate him.
items.why_it_matters: The record supplies detailed, evidence-based examples of presidential pressure on investigators and witnesses. Its force comes from the documented conduct, not from pretending Mueller issued a criminal verdict that he explicitly declined to make.

evidence.label: Report on the Investigation into Russian Interference, Volume II
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

evidence.label: Special Counsel Mueller's May 29, 2019 statement
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

items.editorial_note: Do not write that Mueller found Trump guilty of obstruction. The defensible claim is that the report documented substantial evidence, declined a traditional charging judgment under sitting-president policy, and explicitly did not exonerate him.

items.title: The Government Accountability Office found that the Trump administration illegally withheld Ukraine security assistance
items.status_label: Official GAO finding of an Impoundment Control Act violation
items.summary: The Office of Management and Budget withheld approximately $214 million in congressionally appropriated Defense Department security assistance for Ukraine while the administration pursued a policy objective. On January 16, 2020, the nonpartisan Government Accountability Office concluded that OMB had violated the Impoundment Control Act because the executive branch cannot substitute its policy priorities for Congress's enacted funding decisions.
items.why_it_matters: The finding provides a narrow, formal legal judgment about executive misuse of spending authority at the center of the Ukraine pressure campaign. It is independent of the political judgments reached in impeachment proceedings.

evidence.label: GAO Decision B-331564, Office of Management and Budget—Withholding of Ukraine Security Assistance
evidence.publisher: U.S. Government Accountability Office

items.editorial_note: Attribute the legal violation to OMB/the administration as GAO did. The GAO decision was not a criminal conviction of Trump.

items.title: The House impeached Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress; the Senate acquitted him
items.status_label: Impeached on two articles; acquitted by the Senate on both
items.summary: The House of Representatives impeached Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress after finding that he used presidential authority to pressure Ukraine for investigations that would benefit him politically and then directed defiance of the House inquiry. On February 5, 2020, the Senate voted 48 guilty to 52 not guilty on abuse of power and 47 guilty to 53 not guilty on obstruction, resulting in acquittal on both articles.
items.why_it_matters: The episode joins the Ukraine pressure campaign to a constitutional accountability process. The House judgment was grave and specific, but a complete account must include that the Senate did not reach the two-thirds threshold needed to convict and remove.

evidence.label: H. Res. 755, Articles of Impeachment Against President Donald J. Trump
evidence.publisher: Congress.gov

evidence.label: Senate roll call on Article I, abuse of power
evidence.publisher: U.S. Senate

evidence.label: Senate roll call on Article II, obstruction of Congress
evidence.publisher: U.S. Senate

items.editorial_note: Impeachment is a constitutional accusation by the House, not a criminal conviction. Always pair it with the Senate acquittal and exact vote totals.

items.title: The January 6 committee documented a multi-part effort to overturn the 2020 election
items.status_label: Congressional factual findings and criminal referrals; not a court judgment
items.summary: The House Select Committee's final report assembled testimony, documents, and public evidence describing a sustained effort led by Trump to reverse his election defeat. The report covered false fraud claims, pressure on state officials, a false-elector plan, efforts to enlist the Justice Department, pressure on Vice President Pence, the Ellipse speech, and Trump's failure for hours to stop the Capitol attack. The committee also issued nonbinding criminal referrals.
items.why_it_matters: The report connects episodes that can look isolated into a coherent attempt to remain in power despite the certified result. It is a congressional investigation, however, and its referrals were not convictions or binding prosecutorial findings.

evidence.label: Final Report of the Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
evidence.publisher: U.S. Government Publishing Office

evidence.label: Select Committee criminal referrals and supporting material
evidence.publisher: Congress.gov

items.editorial_note: Use the committee's findings as attributed findings. Do not convert a congressional referral into a conviction or imply that every named participant was adjudicated as part of one conspiracy.

items.title: The federal election-subversion prosecution ended without trial after Trump's reelection
items.status_label: Charges dismissed without prejudice; no trial or merits verdict
items.summary: A federal grand jury indicted Trump over alleged conspiracies and obstruction aimed at overturning the 2020 election. After Trump won the 2024 election, Special Counsel Jack Smith moved to dismiss the case under the Justice Department's longstanding position that a sitting president cannot be federally prosecuted. The court dismissed without prejudice on November 25, 2024. Smith's later report said his office believed the admissible evidence would have been sufficient to convict, but that prosecutorial assessment was never tested at trial.
items.why_it_matters: The case placed detailed election-subversion allegations in a criminal pleading, but reelection—not a jury verdict—terminated the prosecution. Its procedural ending is central to the accountability story and must not be misdescribed as either exoneration or conviction.

evidence.label: Federal court record for United States v. Trump, No. 1:23-cr-257
evidence.publisher: U.S. Government Publishing Office

evidence.label: Special Counsel Smith report, Volume One
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

items.editorial_note: An indictment alleges; it does not prove. Smith's sufficiency assessment is a prosecutor's conclusion, not a verdict. A dismissal without prejudice is not an acquittal, but no current criminal liability was adjudicated in this case.

items.title: Georgia's election-interference prosecution was dismissed in full without a trial
items.status_label: All remaining charges dismissed on the prosecutor's motion; no merits verdict
items.summary: A Fulton County grand jury indicted Trump and 18 co-defendants in August 2023 on racketeering and related charges arising from efforts to change Georgia's 2020 election result. After District Attorney Fani Willis was disqualified and a replacement prosecutor reviewed the case, the state filed a nolle prosequi. On November 26, 2025, Judge Scott McAfee granted the motion and dismissed the case in its entirety. Trump was never tried on the allegations.
items.why_it_matters: The indictment captured conduct including the call asking Georgia's secretary of state to find 11,780 votes, but institutional and procedural breakdowns prevented a merits trial. The dismissal is a major accountability failure or safeguard depending on perspective; either way, it cannot be presented as proof of guilt.

evidence.label: State's motion to enter nolle prosequi and prosecutorial review
evidence.publisher: Prosecuting Attorneys' Council of Georgia

evidence.label: Order granting dismissal of the Georgia election case
evidence.publisher: Fulton County Superior Court via Democracy Docket

items.editorial_note: Say the charges were dismissed, not that Trump was acquitted. Guilty pleas by several former co-defendants do not establish Trump's guilt and should not be transferred to him.

items.title: The House impeached Trump for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him despite a 57-vote guilty majority
items.status_label: Impeached; acquitted because the Senate fell short of a two-thirds vote
items.summary: One week after the Capitol attack, the House impeached Trump for incitement of insurrection, citing his months of false election claims, pressure on Georgia officials, and January 6 speech. On February 13, 2021, 57 senators voted guilty and 43 voted not guilty. That bipartisan majority fell short of the Constitution's two-thirds threshold, so the Senate acquitted him.
items.why_it_matters: The second impeachment was the constitutional branch's most direct response to Trump's role in the attack on the transfer of power. Both facts matter: a majority of senators voted to convict, and the constitutionally required supermajority did not.

evidence.label: H. Res. 24, article of impeachment for incitement of insurrection
evidence.publisher: Congress.gov

evidence.label: Senate roll call on the second impeachment article
evidence.publisher: U.S. Senate

items.editorial_note: Impeachment is not a criminal prosecution. State both the 57-43 vote and the failure to reach the required two-thirds threshold.

items.title: Trump granted sweeping clemency to January 6 defendants on returning to office
items.status_label: Completed lawful exercise of presidential clemency
items.summary: On his first day back in office, Trump commuted the sentences of 14 named January 6 defendants to time served, granted full and unconditional pardons to all other people convicted of January 6-related offenses, and directed the Justice Department to seek dismissal with prejudice of pending indictments. The proclamation was categorical rather than individualized by offense or conduct.
items.why_it_matters: The proclamation used a broad constitutional power to erase or halt federal consequences for the attack that disrupted certification of Trump's election defeat. It sharply reversed the Justice Department's years-long accountability effort and included people convicted of serious violence as well as lower-level offenses.

evidence.label: Presidential proclamation granting January 6 pardons and commutations
evidence.publisher: The White House

evidence.label: Justice Department record of the proclamation
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of the Pardon Attorney

items.editorial_note: The clemency was lawful under the President's constitutional pardon power. Criticism should focus on its breadth, beneficiaries, and accountability consequences, not claim the pardons were themselves criminal.

items.title: The classified-documents indictment was dismissed on appointment grounds and never reached trial
items.status_label: Indictment dismissed; appeal ended as to Trump after reelection; no merits verdict
items.summary: Federal prosecutors charged Trump with willful retention of national-defense information, obstruction, concealment, and false-statement offenses related to documents kept after his presidency. On July 15, 2024, Judge Aileen Cannon dismissed the indictment after concluding that Special Counsel Jack Smith had been appointed in violation of the Appointments Clause. Following Trump's reelection, the Justice Department ended its appeal as to him under sitting-president policy. No jury decided the factual allegations.
items.why_it_matters: The indictment described unusually sensitive records and alleged attempts to obstruct their recovery, but the prosecution failed on a threshold structural issue before trial. The distinction between disturbing allegations and an unadjudicated, dismissed case is indispensable to a credible evidence site.

evidence.label: Superseding indictment in United States v. Trump, Nauta, and De Oliveira
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

evidence.label: Justice Department summary of dismissal and subsequent termination of federal charges
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of Information Policy

items.editorial_note: An indictment is not evidence of guilt by itself. Make clear that the court dismissed on the special counsel's appointment, not after a factual acquittal, and that Trump never faced a merits trial.

items.title: Congressional investigators documented millions in foreign-government payments to Trump businesses while he was president
items.status_label: Payments documented; Emoluments Clause claims not finally adjudicated on the merits
items.summary: A 2024 report from Democratic staff of the House Oversight Committee, based on accounting records produced by Trump's former firm Mazars, identified at least $7.8 million paid by at least 20 foreign governments or state-controlled entities to four Trump businesses during two years of his first term. Earlier constitutional lawsuits over alleged emoluments ended as moot after he left office, with judgments vacated rather than a final Supreme Court merits ruling.
items.why_it_matters: Trump retained ownership of a global business while exercising presidential power, creating recurring channels through which foreign states could spend money at properties benefiting him. The documented receipts establish the conflict mechanism even though courts did not finally decide whether particular transactions violated the Constitution.

evidence.label: House Oversight Democratic staff report, White House for Sale
evidence.publisher: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability, Democratic staff

evidence.label: Supreme Court docket showing vacatur and mootness disposition in Trump v. District of Columbia
evidence.publisher: Supreme Court of the United States

evidence.label: GSA Inspector General evaluation of the Old Post Office lease
evidence.publisher: General Services Administration Office of Inspector General

items.editorial_note: Attribute the $7.8 million figure to the committee's Democratic staff and note that it covered only records obtained from a small portion of Trump's businesses. Do not state that the Supreme Court found an Emoluments Clause violation; it did not reach the merits.

items.title: The administration implemented family separation without adequate planning for children or reunification
items.status_label: Inspector General failures documented; class-action settlement approved
items.summary: The Trump administration's zero-tolerance prosecution policy led to the separation of more than 3,000 children from parents at the southwest border. A Justice Department Inspector General review found that senior leaders understood prosecutions would cause separations but failed to prepare for the consequences, coordinate with partner agencies, or plan reunification. The government later settled the Ms. L. class action, providing reunification and support measures and limiting future separations; the settlement did not award damages.
items.why_it_matters: The record shows that harm to children was a foreseeable consequence of a deterrence-focused policy, not an unforeseeable administrative accident. The failures were documented by the administration's own watchdog and produced continuing court-supervised remedies.

evidence.label: DOJ Inspector General report on planning and implementation of zero tolerance
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General

evidence.label: Justice Department announcement of the Ms. L. family-separation settlement
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

evidence.label: Ms. L. settlement agreement
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice

items.editorial_note: The Inspector General criticized the Justice Department's planning and implementation; it did not enter a criminal judgment. The settlement supplied injunctive relief and services, not class-wide damages.

items.title: A federal judge found Trump's mass inspector-general removals violated statutory notice requirements
items.status_label: Statutory violation found; reinstatement denied and case stayed pending higher-court guidance
items.summary: On January 24, 2025, Trump removed more than a dozen inspectors general without giving Congress the advance notice and substantive, case-specific reasons required by the Inspector General Act. In September 2025, a federal district judge concluded that the removals violated the statute. The court nevertheless denied reinstatement and other permanent injunctive relief and stayed proceedings while related Supreme Court separation-of-powers litigation remained pending.
items.why_it_matters: Inspectors general are an internal check on waste, fraud, abuse, and political retaliation across the executive branch. Removing a large group without legally required notice weakened that check and tested whether statutory accountability safeguards can produce an effective remedy.

evidence.label: District court memorandum opinion in Storch v. Trump
evidence.publisher: U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia via Justia

evidence.label: Congressional Research Service overview of inspector general removal requirements and recent removals
evidence.publisher: Congressional Research Service via Congress.gov

items.editorial_note: The court found a notice violation but did not reinstate the officials. Track later appellate or Supreme Court developments before describing the case as finally resolved.

items.title: The Supreme Court held Trump's birthright-citizenship order unconstitutional
items.status_label: Final Supreme Court merits decision affirming that the executive order is unconstitutional
items.summary: Executive Order 14160 directed federal agencies to deny recognition of citizenship to certain children born in the United States based on their parents' immigration status. On June 30, 2026, the Supreme Court held that children born in the United States to parents who are unlawfully present or temporarily present are citizens at birth under the Fourteenth Amendment and federal law, and affirmed judgment against the order.
items.why_it_matters: The order attempted by unilateral executive action to narrow a constitutional citizenship guarantee affecting children born on U.S. soil. The Supreme Court's merits ruling supplies a definitive example of presidential action exceeding constitutional limits.

evidence.label: Executive Order 14160, Protecting the Meaning and Value of American Citizenship
evidence.publisher: The White House

evidence.label: Trump v. Barbara, Supreme Court opinion
evidence.publisher: Supreme Court of the United States

items.editorial_note: This entry reflects a June 30, 2026 merits ruling and should not be confused with the Court's earlier procedural ruling about nationwide injunctions.

items.title: Trump pardoned political allies and associates convicted in cases connected to investigations of his circle
items.status_label: Completed lawful exercise of presidential clemency
items.summary: In the closing weeks of his first term, Trump pardoned former national security adviser Michael Flynn, longtime adviser Roger Stone, former campaign chairman Paul Manafort, and Charles Kushner, the father of Trump's son-in-law. The recipients' cases included false statements, obstruction, witness tampering, tax and bank fraud, conspiracy, and campaign-connected conduct. The pardons were constitutionally valid and ended or relieved federal punishment.
items.why_it_matters: The pattern raises a conflict-of-interest question: a president used personal clemency power to benefit allies, a campaign chairman, an adviser who resisted an investigation, and a close relative of his family. That is a legitimate abuse-of-power critique even without claiming the pardons were illegal.

evidence.label: Pardons granted by President Donald J. Trump, 2017-2021
evidence.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of the Pardon Attorney

items.editorial_note: A pardon is lawful and does not by itself prove a corrupt bargain. Ground criticism in the recipients' relationships to Trump, their adjudicated offenses, and the accountability consequences.
